name: The rich young ruler
notes: A threefold synoptic account of the man who asks how to inherit eternal life, told by Matthew, Mark, and Luke. The shared core runs through the same beads: the address "Good Master," the reply about who alone is good, the commandments, the man's claim to have kept them, the call to sell all and follow with treasure in heaven, his sorrowful departure for his great riches, the saying on how hardly the rich enter the kingdom, the camel and the needle's eye, "Who then can be saved," the answer that what is impossible with men is possible with God, Peter's "we have left all," and the promise of manifold reward and eternal life. Each writer keeps complementary vantage: Matthew names him a young man and adds the twelve thrones and "what lack I yet," Mark alone records that Jesus beholding him loved him and bids him take up the cross, and Luke alone calls him a ruler. Matthew and Mark close with "many that are first shall be last," which Luke leaves in place without that closing line. The detail that Matthew has "what good thing shall I do" while Mark and Luke have "what shall I do that I may inherit eternal life" is left as a matter for further study.
